text: What's a belief or maybe a behavior that
you had, let's say 10 or 15 years ago
that you now see as misguided?
>> Uh
not committing.
>> Not committing. Interesting. Tell me
more about Say more about that.
>> So, I mean, I I define commitment as the
elimination of alternatives, which was I
had the misconception that I will work
on multiple things and see which one
works. When the reality, any of them
would have worked if I had worked on it.
And so, I would just never get past the
point of critical mass. And so, the
easiest analogy I have for this is
imagine you
uh want to start a party, right? It's
very hard to start a good party. Like,
you have to invite a lot of people, you
have to prepare, you have to do all the
stuff.
Um but if you are successful enough to
get enough people to the party, the
party can eventually take off on its own
and kind of become its own thing. Now,
to the same degree, imagine you try to
start four parties on the same night at
the same time.
It's almost impossible. Because you only
have the only have the same resource.
You have the same number of people you
know, you have the same amount of money.
It's very, very hard to do that. And so,
this is what I see the vast majority of,
you know, smaller entrepreneurs uh doing
is that they're trying to start four
parties and say, "Well, whatever party
takes off is the one that I'm going to
I'm going to invest more in." But none
of them will take off. And that's why in
the beginning it feels very manual, very
like pushy. Like, "I promise the party's
going to be great. It's going to be
awesome. But who else is coming?"
No, you. No one, right? But if you just
put all of You're like, if you're like,
"I'll be there. I'm not doing anything
else. This is all I'm going to be doing
is trying to make sure this party's
amazing." The likelihood that party one
is good and then two gets good enough
that you don't have to push to keep
growing it is good. It's just kind of a
microcosm for a business just on a much,
you know, uh shrunken time scale. But I
think for that reason, that's that is
probably the number one reason that
people don't get what they want is
because they spread themselves too thin.
You have such limited resources in the
beginning. It's like I think this is a
piece of people misunderstand. Like, you
have
functionally no money. If you're an
individual, you have basically no money
when it comes to a corporate scale,
right? You have almost no money. And you
have almost no time. Uh again, compared
to 100 people. Right. You have no no
money, no time. And so, you have such
such such limited resources that the
actual skill that's missing is strategy.
And so the strategy is prioritization of
resources. So said simply, like what do
we do and more importantly what do we
not do with the few things that we can
do out of the few things that we can do.
And so
just being able to just say, you know
what? All four of these parties could be
good. It could be a pirate's theme, it
could be a it could be a Greek theme, it
could be a you know, whatever theme. But
I'm just going to try and make this
pirate theme one good. That's all I'm
going to do. And the thing is is that we
then think, oh, well, this succeeded
because it was a pirate theme. No, it
just succeeded because that was the one
that you worked on. So as long as
fundamentally like you let people know
about your stuff, you have a conversion
mechanism, and you have and the thing
that you sell cost significantly less
than the thing you sell at the price you
sell it for, you'll probably be okay.
But people just spend forever trying to
figure out which party theme is going to
be the best theme.
And you just don't need to do that
because as soon as you pick, you
eliminate the alternatives, you commit,
and then you're good to go.

revenue,
uh between like
nine different businesses.

>> And I was 20 26
at the time.
Uh and I like to tell people I, you

because I thought that's what successful

like, no, you make one business
extremely successful and then you just
make investments and you don't spend

got DUI, hit on collision, whole thing.
Like I like yeah, and then I had
somebody uh thankfully say, hey, uh you
were stretched too thin. This stress
will kill you quite literally and you
need to make decisions. The difficulty
was for me, I was the rainmaker in all
the businesses. And so I basically was
just killing myself to generate income
for all of them. Um and almost nothing

to pay people to do everything else
because that was the only thing that I

chance because you're only giving 2

>> Yeah.
>> Uh, what if you spent all your time on

worked for years to build these
businesses and like, what are you

you sell them for more money?" Um, and I
>> And you walked out tomorrow, right?

fire sold it all. What was really
interesting is that over the next 6

businesses
uh, put together by just focusing on one

because the thing is is that focus never
gets easier.
It's kind of like an addiction. Like,
you have to beat it every day.
Real quick, if you are a business owner
and you are not growing as fast as you'd
like, I'd like to give you a free gift.
So, my team and I put together the $100
scaling roadmap, which is basically 200
hours of us looking over all the
portfolio companies we've had and what
stages of growth they went through and
more importantly, where they got stuck
and how they got past it. And so, we
broke it into these 10 stages and we

where if you put in your business
